interactions.summary: Jiang opens the U.S.-Iran war analysis with three testable questions: whether the United States will launch a ground invasion, whether nuclear weapons will be used, and whether the Al-Aqsa Mosque will be destroyed. He then introduces the standard escalation-dominance theory and says the lecture will show why mere dominance is the wrong way to read the war.

speaker_notes.note: Single continuous Jiang lecture; rhetorical questions are lecture framing rather than audience questions.

claims.claim: Jiang predicts that if the United States launches a ground invasion of Iran, the war will escalate quickly and trap the United States for five to ten years, making the loss catastrophic whether it wins or loses.
claims.temporal_scope: Prediction made on 2026-03-10 about the U.S.-Iran war then under discussion.

claims.claim: A U.S. ground war in Iran would require a national draft that sends men as young as eighteen into the army.
Jiang says nuclear use remains a geopolitical taboo; Israeli tactical nuclear use would break the post-World-War-II taboo and risk nuclear apocalypse.
He identifies Al-Aqsa as the third key war question because its destruction would religiously obligate the Muslim world, in his account, to war against Israel.
Jiang makes three explicit game-theory predictions: U.S. ground troops yes, nuclear weapons no, and Al-Aqsa destruction yes; he says the validity of the theory depends on all three being correct.
The dominant escalation-ladder theory says the actor with escalation dominance has the advantage, but Jiang rejects this theory as incomplete for the U.S.-Iran war.
In Jiang’s street-fight model, escalation is not just the two fighters; spectators, friends, police, and God become audiences before whom each move must be justified.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang stakes the lecture on falsifiability: if one of the three predictions misses, the whole analysis is wrong.
signature_moments.source_phrase: If I miss one, then all my theory is wrong
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This makes the episode unusually explicit as a test of Jiang’s game-theory method.

signature_moments.moment: The nuclear gun looks dominant, but Jiang says the theory built around the gun is already wrong.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It sets up the central reversal from escalation dominance to escalation control.

signature_moments.moment: Even God becomes part of the game tree because a dead fighter still has to explain how he died.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The image makes legitimacy and justification part of escalation mechanics.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: A stepwise model of conflict escalation from minor conflict through violence to higher weapons or stakes.
The conventional idea that the actor able to climb higher on the escalation ladder has the advantage.
A small military commitment expanding gradually into a larger war, with Vietnam as Jiang’s example.

chronology_notes.note: This lecture gives a dated March 10, 2026 prediction set for later comparison against subsequent U.S.-Iran war episodes.
